title: Measuring anyon dispersion with tunneling probes
abstract: Anyons are usually characterized by their topological data and their fractional quantum numbers under global symmetries. In lattice systems such as fractional Chern insulators (FCI), they are also mobile quasiparticles. Their motion controls the possible ground states of the dilute anyon gas obtained by doping an FCI, including possible superconducting states. We show how tunneling probes can measure this motion. In scanning tunneling spectroscopy, weak disorder produces spatially oscillating quasiparticle-interference patterns whose branches reveal the dispersion of fractionalized constituents. In quantum twisting microscopy, planar momentum-conserving tunneling selects the total momentum of the injected electron, so the continuum thresholds of fractionalized electron spectra encode the dispersion of the constituent anyons. The resulting spectra distinguish compact electron-like excitations, bound anyon molecules, and unbound anyon continuum.
summary: This paper details methods to spectroscopically measure the dispersion of anyons, fractionalized quasiparticles, in topological materials like Fractional Chern Insulators. It utilizes both Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (QPI) and Quantum Twisting Microscopy (QTM) to analyze the singularity structure of tunneling spectra. The distinct power laws observed in these measurements allow researchers to differentiate and quantify the energy and momentum characteristics of various anyon excitation types.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop spectroscopic techniques to measure the dispersion relation of anyons, which are fractionalized quasiparticles, in topological systems like Fractional Chern Insulators (FCI).
main_result: By analyzing the power-law singularities in quasiparticle interference (QPI) patterns and measuring fixed-momentum thresholds via quantum twisting microscopy (QTM), the distinct dispersions of compact excitations, bound anyon molecules, and unbound continua can be separated and quantified.
method: The study combines theoretical derivations of weak-scattering singularities using Green's functions with analysis of experimental signatures from Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (STS) and Quantum Twisting Microscopy (QTM).
model_or_system: The physical system under study is the Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) or related topological matter, which hosts anyons. The analysis models the low-energy dispersion of these mobile anyons, which dictate the ground state of doped systems.
key_observables: The primary observables are the power-law exponents of the singularity structure in the differential conductance ($	ext{d}I/	ext{d}V$) and the fixed-momentum thresholds measured by QTM.
important_parameters:
Key parameters include the topological filling fraction ($
u$), the momentum transfer ($q$), and the energy detuning ($\omega - E_{branch}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on local expansions near isolated minima and assumes that matrix-element constraints do not alter the fundamental on-shell branch points.
figures_summary: FIG. 1 illustrates the QPI geometry; FIG. 2 shows convolution kernels for bound and continuum sectors; FIG. 3 details the QTM setup and spectral features for the three-anyon continuum; TABLE I and TABLE II summarize the expected power laws for different excitation sectors.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the physical context (anyons in FCI), then detailing the theoretical framework for analyzing spectral singularities (QPI/QTM), deriving the specific mathematical forms for different excitation types (e, ba, aaa), and finally presenting the comparative analysis of the two measurement techniques.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful experimental roadmap for characterizing exotic quasiparticles in strongly correlated topological phases, linking abstract topological concepts (anyons) to measurable spectroscopic signatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. STS-QPI geometry and a fixed-q line cut. (a) An electron injected from the STM tip can enter the FCI as an aaa continuum of three charge-e/3 anyons. A weak impu- rity transfers momentum q to one constituent, and the inter- ference between amplitudes with and without this scattering produces the q component of the conductance map. (b) Nu- merical fixed-q line cut for the aaa sector. The first dashed line marks the clean activation threshold 3∆a, while the sec- ond marks the on-shell QPI branch ωaaa(q). The indicated powers are those of ∂2 V (dI/dV ) for Laughlin ν = 1/3.

FIG. 2. Fourier-transform STS signals from the full numerical convolution kernel. (a) dI/dV for the ba sector. The two dashed guides correspond to scattering b and scattering a. (b) First energy derivative of the same ba signal. (c) dI/dV for the aaa sector. (d) Second energy derivative of the aaa signal. The dashed guide is ωaaa(q) = 3∆a + q2/(8Ma).

FIG. 3. QTM. (a) A monolayer graphene (MLG) QTM probe forms a planar tunnel junction with the FCI sam- ple. The twist angle selects the injected electron momentum K. (b) dI/dV and ∂2 V (dI/dV ) for the aaa contribution to A+ e,FCI(K, eV ) in the small-pocket, elastic-tunneling limit.
